coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and phosphorus dual doped-fluorescent carbon quantum dots: An efficient nanoprobe sensor for sensitive and selective detection of ferric and mercuric ions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yadav, Rajnee
Lahariya, Vikas
Singh, Akhilesh Kumar
coredata.dc:description: The work uses phosphorous and nitrogen-co-doped carbon quantum dots (PN-CQDs) to present the effective and precise metal ion sensing for Fe (III) and Hg (II) in aqueous environments. Fluorescent PN-CQDs were synthesized using a microwave method with Citrus limetta juice as the carbon source, phosphoric acid (85 %) as the phosphorus source, and ethylenediamine for nitrogen. Structural analysis confirmed 4 nm spherical particles with an amorphous carbon core and rich surface functionalities. XPS revealed 6.63 % phosphorus and 15.48 % nitrogen doping. Optical studies showed excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) in the UV–visible range, with maximum emission at 550 nm under 440 nm excitation. The PN-CQDs exhibited a high quantum yield (56 %) and strong photostability under UV and visible light. For heavy metal ion sensing, the PN-CQDs showed high selectivity towards Hg(II) and sensitivity for Fe(III) among other metal ions, achieving nanomolar detection limits (1.35 nM for Fe(III) and 5.23 nM for Hg(II)). The linear response range and low detection limits highlight the potential of PN-CQDs as reliable fluorescent probes for the selective detection of Fe(III) and Hg(II) metal ions. The quenching mechanism was attributed to static quenching through ground state complex formation. The presence of phosphorus and pyridinic-N groups enabled selective Fe(III) and Hg(II) detection in real water samples with reliable recovery rates (96–100 %). These results underscore the high sensitivity and selectivity of PN-CQDs for detecting Fe(III) and Hg(II) ions, making them highly effective for environmental monitoring and bioanalytical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phosphorous
Nitrogen
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence
Heavy metal ion sensing
Quantum yield

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image with particle size distribution histogram in inset and (b) XRD diffractogram of PN-CQDs.
(a) XPS wide scan spectrum and high-resolution scan of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) P2p of PN-CQDs respectively.
(a) Raman spectra in deconvoluted form and (b) FTIR transmittance pattern of PN-CQDs.
(a) UV–visible absorbance spectrum with corresponding emission spectrum and (b) CIE 1931 plot for PN-CQDs.

PL emission spectrum of PN-CQDs with (a) different excitation wavelength, (b) pH variation, (c) under UV and Visible light illumination, and (d) in the presence of different concentrations of Na(I) & K(I) ions.
(a) The sensitivity and (b) selectivity study of PN-CQDs.

(a & c) PL emission spectra of PN-CQDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe(III) & Hg(II), (b) PN-CQDs real time image under UV in absence and presence of ferric & mercuric ions, and (d & e) PL intensity ratio (F/Fo) at 440 nm versus concentration of Fe(III) and Hg(II) ions.
(a) TRPL spectrum and (b) Zeta potential of PN-CQDs.
PL intensity of PN-CQDs with DI water, well water, pond water, and sea water as solvent.
Excitation and corresponding emission wavelengths and CIE co-ordinates of PN-CQDs.
List of P & N co-doped carbon-based quantum dots constructed FL sensor for Fe(III) and Hg(II) along with present work.

row.entry: Diaminobenzene sulfonic acid, phenylboronic acid, and phosphoric acid
Butene dioic acid, p-phenylenediamine, and phosphoric acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of Fe (III) and Hg(II) ions recovery experiments conducted with well, pond, and sea water.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and phosphorus dual doped-fluorescent carbon quantum dots: An efficient nanoprobe sensor for sensitive and selective detection of ferric and mercuric ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Amity School of Applied Sciences, Amity University Haryana, 122413, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Amity School of Applied Sciences, Amity University Haryana, 122413, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physical Sciences, Banasthali Vidhyapith, Banasthali, Rajasthan 304022, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physical Sciences, Banasthali Vidhyapith, Banasthali, Rajasthan 304022, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The work uses phosphorous and nitrogen-co-doped carbon quantum dots (PN-CQDs) to present the effective and precise metal ion sensing for Fe (III) and Hg (II) in aqueous environments. Fluorescent PN-CQDs were synthesized using a microwave method with Citrus limetta juice as the carbon source, phosphoric acid (85 %) as the phosphorus source, and ethylenediamine for nitrogen. Structural analysis confirmed 4 nm spherical particles with an amorphous carbon core and rich surface functionalities. XPS revealed 6.63 % phosphorus and 15.48 % nitrogen doping. Optical studies showed excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) in the UV–visible range, with maximum emission at 550 nm under 440 nm excitation. The PN-CQDs exhibited a high quantum yield (56 %) and strong photostability under UV and visible light. For heavy metal ion sensing, the PN-CQDs showed high selectivity towards Hg(II) and sensitivity for Fe(III) among other metal ions, achieving nanomolar detection limits (1.35 nM for Fe(III) and 5.23 nM for Hg(II)). The linear response range and low detection limits highlight the potential of PN-CQDs as reliable fluorescent probes for the selective detection of Fe(III) and Hg(II) metal ions. The quenching mechanism was attributed to static quenching through ground state complex formation. The presence of phosphorus and pyridinic-N groups enabled selective Fe(III) and Hg(II) detection in real water samples with reliable recovery rates (96–100 %). These results underscore the high sensitivity and selectivity of PN-CQDs for detecting Fe(III) and Hg(II) ions, making them highly effective for environmental monitoring and bioanalytical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All the data used in the study are incorporated in the manuscript.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal ion contamination is a pressing environmental issue that poses significant threats to ecosystems and human health. Heavy metals such as lead (Pb(II)), cadmium (Cd(II)), mercuric (Hg(II)), and ferric (Fe(III)) are among the most hazardous pollutants due to their toxicity, persistence, and ability to bioaccumulate in living organisms. These contaminants can originate from various sources, including industrial discharges, agricultural runoff, mining activities, and improper waste disposal. Among the heavy metals, Fe (III) and Hg (II) ions are particularly concerning. Ferric ions, while essential in trace amounts for biological functions, can become toxic at elevated concentrations, leading to oxidative stress, cellular damage, and various health issues such as nervous, liver and kidney damage [1]. Mostly surface water contains iron in Fe(III) form. Mercuric ions are highly toxic even at low concentrations, affecting the central nervous system and leading to severe neurological and developmental disorders [2]. Given their toxicity and prevalence, efficient and accurate detection and monitoring of Fe(III) and Hg(II) in environmental samples are crucial.

Conventional methods for detecting metal ions, such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS), atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), and electrochemical techniques, are known for their high precision and sensitivity. However, they typically demand advanced equipment, considerable sample preparation, and skilled operators. Additionally, these methods tend to be time-intensive and costly, limiting their practicality for quick, on-site testing. Meanwhile fluorescence sensing has emerged as a powerful alternative for detecting metal ions, other toxic chemicals and pollutants due to its high sensitivity, selectivity, simplicity, and rapid response. This method able to detect minute changes or increasing contamination in the soil, water and environment, providing real-time monitoring capabilities. Additionally, fluorescence-based methods can be easily miniaturized and integrated into portable devices, making them ideal for in-field applications [3,4].

CQDs have attracted significant attention for heavy metal ion sensing based on their efficient fluorometric nature biocompatibility, chemical inertness, anti-photo bleaching and environmental friendliness [5]. The CQDs have been shown the superior performance in various applications, including bioimaging [6], drug delivery, environmental monitoring, and anticounterfeiting etc. [7]. All above-mentioned properties are inclined with rich surface functionality and more energy-active sites based on the chemical bonding and surface reactivity. It can also be achieved by doping and co-doping with the non-metal ions. The fluorescence characteristics of CQDs are intricately linked to the dopant level. Doping enhance CQDs' fluorescence efficiency by introducing the defect states and facilitating the formation of conjugated carbon clusters within the graphite core. Recent advancements in doped CQDs and their nanocomposites have been focused on enhancing their properties through doping with heteroatoms such as nitrogen (N) phosphorus (P), sulfur (S), and boron (B) [8]. In all these, nitrogen and phosphorus, both elements of the same group in the periodic table, have similar outer shell electronic structures and the same number of valence electrons (+5). Nitrogen is commonly used as a doping element in the synthesis of CQDs due to its atomic radius being close to that of carbon and its ability to form strong bonds with carbon atoms through its five valence electrons. Doping in CQDs with nitrogen transforms them into n-type donors. Phosphorus, having a relatively larger atomic radius to carbon as a dopant in CQDs, also acts as an n-type donor and induces substitutional energy states in sp3-bonded carbon core structure. The co-doping of CQDs with both N and P (PN-CQDs) has shown synergistic enhancements in photoluminescence (PL) and linear optical properties [9]. This dual n-doping creates more surface states that act as active sites, generating new energy levels and enhancing the PL properties of the CQDs. Additionally, the introduction of new functional groups containing nitrogen and phosphorus on the surface of CQDs reduces non-radiative transitions, improves the quantum yield (QY), and prevents aggregation due to increased steric hindrance. These co-doped CQDs have been successfully applied in areas such as sensing, photocatalysis, and bioimaging, showcasing their versatility and enhanced performance [10].

In the past, N, P co-doped CQDs with tunable photoluminescence properties have been utilized for sensing and detection of heavy metal ions, pH, toxin pollutants and the biomedical field [9,11,12]. Cao et al. demonstrated green and blue emitting fluorescent N, P co-doped carbon dots with tunable emission, suitable for bioimaging, sensing, and drug delivery applications [13]. In addition, the significant potential of N,P co-doped CQDs for environmental remediation was also found. For example, Liu et al. reported that N & P co-doped CQDs showed remarkable improvements in fluorescence intensity and stability, crucial for the photodegradation of methylene blue dye [14]. Yashwanth et al. found enhanced photocatalytic properties in nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped CQDs in comparison to individually doped CQDs [9]. In a study, N, P co-doped CDs exhibited strong fluorescence emission at 626 nm and showed notably enhanced the plant’s photosynthetic efficiency to water spinach using root and leaf spraying techniques [11]. Bao et al. explored the potential of Eleocharis dulcis-derived N, P co-doped CQDs in anti-counterfeiting applications, and showcased efficacy in Fe3+ ion detection, with a linear detection range between 50 and 350 µM and a detection limit of 0.56 µM [12]. Similarly, Babar et al. reported N, P co-doped CQDs for the detection of toxin compound trinitrophenol (TNP), with remarkable quenching sensitivity and a detection limit as low as 23 µM, demonstrating their high specificity towards TNP [15]. In a similar case, hazardous Doxorubicin (DOX) was detected through N & P co-doped carbon dots by Zhang et al. with a 0.012 µM detection limit [16]. Improving the applicability and accuracy of prepared CQDs for heavy metal ions detection is the prime concern of the research work. Hence, nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped CQDs have demonstrated significant advancements over individually doped CQDs, as highlighted by several recent studies. In addition, our recent work demonstrated the ability of highly sensitive detection of ferric and mercuric metal ions using N-doped carbon dots and P-doped carbon dots separately using citrus limetta juice carbon precursor [17,18]. Among these two studies, N-doped carbon dots presented remarkably lower detection limits to the nanomolar range.

With the motivation of the previously published work and our recent outcomes, here we studied the fluorometric sensing application PN-CQDs for tracing the heavy metal ions (Fe(III) and Hg(II)) and demonstrated its employability for actual water samples exist in our surrounding. The prepared PN-CQDs present high quantum yields (56 %), photostability, and sensitivity towards ferric and mercuric ions compared to our earlier work with N- and P-doped CQDs individually [17,18]. The significance of my research lies in the application of PN-CQDs for dual metal ion sensing, specifically targeting Fe(III) and Hg(II) ions and achieving detection limits (LOD) of 1.35 nM for Fe(III) and 5.23 nM for Hg(II).

In this study, we report the PN-CQDs derived from citrus limita juice using a microwave-assisted method. The use of sweet lime juice as a carbon source presents an eco-friendly, cost-effective alternative to traditional carbon precursors, while microwave-assisted synthesis offers a rapid and energy-efficient approach to CQD production. Additionally, the use of microwave irradiation for synthesis significantly reduces the reaction time to just 5 min, compared to the longer durations required for hydrothermal methods. This not only enhances the efficiency of the process but also aligns with green chemistry principles by minimizing energy consumption.

All the chemicals used in this study were of analytical grade (AR) and employed without further purification. Milli-Q water was utilized for all sample preparations. Locally sourced sweet lime fruits served as the carbon source. For phosphorus doping, phosphoric acid (H3PO3, 85 %) from Merck India was used, while Ethylenediamine (EDA) from Fisher Scientific was employed for nitrogen doping. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and nitric acid (HNO3) were used to adjust the pH.

In the selectivity study, various metal salts were employed, including Copper (II) Chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O), Manganese (II) Chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2·4H2O), Chromium (III) Chloride hexahydrate (CrCl3·6H2O), Zinc Chloride (ZnCl2), Barium Chloride (BaCl2), Silver Nitrate (AgNO3), Magnesium Nitrate (Mg(NO3)2), Calcium Nitrate (Ca(NO3)2), Lithium Chloride (LiCl), Sodium Chloride (NaCl), and Potassium Chloride (KCl), all sourced from CDH. Additionally, Ferric Nitrate (Fe (NO3)3) and Mercuric Chloride (HgCl2) from Fisher Scientific were utilized for the titration studies in metal ion sensing applications.

The preparation method is adopted from our earlier work done on P-doped CQDs [17]. The concentration of N is chosen based on our recently reported study on N-doped carbon dots [18]. In a standard experimental procedure, 20 mL of citrus limita juice is combined with 2 mL of concentrated phosphoric acid and 12.5 mg of ethylenediamine. The mixture is then stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, the entire solution is subjected to a 700 W microwave for 5 min. This process yields a dark brown solution, which is then centrifuged at 14,000 rpm for 10 min to remove larger particles. Then it is filtered by a nylon syringe filter of 0.22 µm pore size. The final colloidal product is used as it is for all subsequent studies.

The detection of mercuric and ferric ions was performed at room temperature using a fluorescence quenching technique. Initially, a 1 µM stock solution was prepared by dissolving a calculated amount of each heavy metal in 100 mL of distilled water. Then, 10 µL of this stock solution was added to 2 mL of the PN-CQDs colloidal solution, and the emission intensity at 440 nm was measured. To further investigate the quenching effect, a constant volume (10 µL) of the metal solution was incrementally added to the same PN-CQDs solution. For comparison, 10 µL of mercuric, ferric ion solutions, and other heavy metal ions (all at 1 µM) were added to separate 2 mL aliquots of the PN-CQDs stock solution, and the emission intensity at 440 nm was recorded.

Water samples from the Bay of Bengal, the local pond (Rewari district), and the university’s tap were collected and subjected to centrifuge at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove heavier unwanted substances. Then supernatant was collected and used for assessment. 1 mL of water samples were added into 2 mL of PN-CQDs, and fluorescence intensity was noted. Further water samples were spiked with heavy metal under study to assess the accuracy of the experimental Stren-Volmer plot.

The morphology of the samples was analysed using a JEOL JEM 2100F Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM), operated at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a Bruker D8 Advance ECO P-XRD, employing Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) for the analysis. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using a Shimadzu ESCA-3400 spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan) equipped with a monochromatic MgKα X-ray source (1253.6 eV) and operating at 10 kV and 10 mA. Charge neutrality was maintained during each run by employing low-energy Ar+ ions and electrons. The Agilent Cary 630 FTIR spectrometer was utilized to record infrared spectra over a range of 4000 cm−1 to 500 cm−1. The UV–visible absorption spectrum was assessed using an Agilent UV–visible spectrophotometer across a wavelength range of 200 nm to 800 nm. Finally, photon-induced light emission was analysed with a Perkin Elmer LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer, covering the wavelength range from 300 nm to 700 nm.

To investigate the surface morphology and distribution of particle sizes of the synthesized PN-CQDs, TEM examinations were conducted, as depicted in Fig. 1 (a). The TEM image vividly portrays that the majority of the CQDs exhibit a spherical configuration and are distinctly separated from each other. Moreover, a histogram illustrating the distribution of particle sizes of PN-CQDs is presented in the inset of Fig. 1(a). The size distribution analysis reveals that the particle sizes of PN-CQDs span from 2 nm to 10 nm. The mean diameter of the PN-CQDs was calculated to be 4.3 ± 0.14 nm. It is noteworthy that the prepared sample showcases a uniform and narrow distribution of sizes and a consistent morphology. The observed particle sizes of PN-CQDs consistently remain below 10 nm, indicating that the samples are situated within the range characteristic of quantum dots.

The obtained XRD peak at 23.5° reflects the structural characteristics of the synthesized material, providing valuable insights into its crystallinity and lattice parameters. The XRD peak at 23.5° reveals the crystallinity and lattice parameters of the synthesized material. Similar to previous reports on carbon dots, the broad peak indicates a disordered or amorphous structure [19–22]. A band around 22° in polymer dots (PDs) suggests weak graphitic crystallinity, while a broad peak at 20.51° in CQDs points to increased amorphousness due to oxygen-containing groups [19,20]. Thus, the observed peak at 23.5° aligns with the general trend observed in carbon-based materials, where broad reflections at similar angles indicate disorder in the structure. The clear reflections at 2θ = 23 and 43° in carbon dots reported by Penki et al., along with the shallow reflection at 72°, collectively depict a disordered nature [21].

The presence of various elements such as carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and phosphorus were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 2 (a) represents a wide scan indicating the presence of available elements in the prepared sample. The given spectra displayed prominent peak of O1s at 534.6 eV, N1s peak at 398.6 eV, C1s peak at 285.6 eV, P2s and P2p peaks at 191.6 and 135.6 eV respectively. Analysis of peak intensity from the survey spectrum given in Fig. 2(a) shows concentrations of oxygen, carbon, phosphorus, and nitrogen are 48.79 %, 29.09 %, 15.48 %, and 6.63 % respectively. Furthermore, Fig. 2(b, c, & d) shows high-resolution scan for C1s, N1s, and P2p. The C1s peak comprises two peaks at 288.1 and 285.9 eV corresponding to the CO and C–O bonds. The deconvolution of P2p peak show peaks positioned at 136.6 and 135.4 eV representing P–C and P–O bonds respectively [17]. Whereas N1s scan has single peak at 398.6 eV corresponding to pyridinic N [23].

The core structure of PN-CQDs was thoroughly analysed using Raman spectroscopy and Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy to understand the structural and functional characteristics of the synthesized CQDs. The Raman spectroscopy given in Fig. 3 (a) revealed three prominent peaks at 1332 cm−1, 1457 cm−1, and 1614 cm−1. These peaks correspond to the D, D', and G bands, respectively, which are characteristic of carbon-based materials [22,24]. The D band, observed at 1332 cm−1, is indicative of the presence of disordered carbon structures or defects within the graphitic layers of the carbon quantum dots. This band is typically associated with the breathing modes of sp2 atoms in rings, signifying the presence of disorder and defects in the carbon lattice. The D' band, located at 1457 cm−1, further emphasizes the high level of disorder within the PN-CQDs. The presence of this band suggests significant disruption in the sp2 hybridized carbon network, which can be attributed to the incorporation of various functional groups and the amorphous nature of the carbon matrix. The higher intensity of the D' band in comparison to the D band provides insight into the structural imperfections. The G band, observed at 1614 cm−1, corresponds to the E2g mode of sp2 bonded carbon atoms, typically seen in graphitic materials. This band represents the in-plane stretching vibrations of the sp2 carbon pairs, signifying the presence of graphitic domains within the CQDs. The position on the higher wavenumber side and low intensity of the G band give information about the acute size of graphitic domains and the low level of graphitization in PN-CQDs.

The ratio of the intensities of the D and G bands, ID/IG is 0.75 which is commonly used to estimate the degree of disorder and the size of the sp2 domains in carbon-based materials. As ID/IG ratio is higher suggesting a higher degree of disorder and smaller graphitic domains in PN-CQDs. This high disorder is further confirmed by the pronounced D' band. Additionally, the presence of the D' band alongside the D and G bands provides evidence of complex structural characteristics, including the presence of edge defects, functional groups, and possibly even the incorporation of P and N atoms within the carbon lattice. This structural complexity is likely a result of the synthesis process, which involves the use of sweet lime juice, phosphoric acid, and ethylenediamine, leading to the formation of highly functionalized and disordered carbon quantum dots.

The FTIR analysis given in Fig. 3(b) provided further insights into the functional groups present on the surface of the PN-CQDs. The transmission peaks were observed at 654, 884, 1003, 1070, 1170, 1522, 1729, 2377, and 2921 cm−1, indicating various functional groups [25]. The 654 cm−1 peak is attributed to out-of-plane bending vibrations of C–H bonds, indicating the presence of aromatic structures within the PN-CQDs. The peak at 884 cm−1 associated with aromatic C–H bending, supporting the presence of aromatic rings in the carbon structure. The 1003 cm−1 peak corresponds to P-O-C vibrations, while the 1070 cm−1 peak indicates the presence of C-O-C and P-O stretching vibrations. The 1170 and 1522 cm−1 peaks are associated with C-N stretching vibrations and N–H bending vibrations, indicating the presence of amine or amide groups, consistent with the use of ethylenediamine in the synthesis. Further 2377 cm−1 peak is associated with CN stretching, indicating the possible presence of nitrile groups. Moreover, 1729 and 2921 cm−1 peaks are attributed to CO and C–H stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of carbonyl groups such as ketones, aldehydes, or carboxylic acids and aliphatic hydrocarbons. The FTIR results complement the Raman spectroscopy findings by providing detailed information on the surface functionalities of the PN-CQDs. The presence of various functional groups, such as carbonyls, hydroxyls, amines, and nitriles, enhances the solubility, reactivity, and potential applications of the CQDs in areas such as sensing, catalysis, and biomedical imaging.

The UV–Visible absorption spectrum of the PN-CQDs exhibited two prominent absorbance peaks at 208 nm and 282 nm (Fig. 4 ). The absorbance peak at 208 nm is attributed to the π-π* transitions of the aromatic CC bonds. The π-π* transitions are typically observed in aromatic systems and confirm the presence of aromatic rings within the core structure of the PN-CQDs [15]. The absorbance peak at 282 nm is associated with the n-π* transitions, which involve the excitation of electrons from non-bonding orbitals to anti-bonding π* orbitals. This peak is often attributed to the presence of carbonyl groups (CO) and other oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CQDs [11]. The presence of such groups was corroborated by the FTIR analysis, which showed transmission peaks at 1070 cm−1 and 1729 cm−1, corresponding to C–O and CO stretching vibrations, respectively. The n-π* transition peak at 282 nm suggests that the PN-CQDs have surface functional groups that contribute to their optical properties and enhance their solubility and reactivity.

The combination of π-π* and n-π* transitions observed in the UV–Visible spectrum highlights the involvement of both aromatic domains and various functional groups on the optical properties. It is crucial for optical absorption and fluorescence properties used in metal ion sensing, photocatalytic, bioimaging, and photonic applications.

The photoluminescence (PL) properties of the PN-CQDs were investigated by varying the excitation wavelength and pH of the colloidal solution. For each excitation wavelength, the chromaticity coordinates for the Commission Internationale De L'Eclairage (CIE) 1931 were also established (the plot is shown in Fig. 4(b)). The variation of excitation wavelength from 270 nm to 530 nm in 30 nm increments exhibited maximum PL intensity at an excitation wavelength of 440 nm and then started to decrease as shown in Fig. 5 (a). In addition, it can be found from the Table 1 that on the excitation wavelength from 390 nm to 440 nm, small variation in the emission wavelength although intensity is in progression. Maximum intensity and narrow band emission is obtained for 440 nm excitation wavelength. The details of the excitation-dependent PL emission spectra are summarized in the Table 1. The emission spectrum covers the entire visible region of light, and a red shift in the emission wavelength was observed with increasing excitation wavelength.

The observed red shift in the emission wavelength with increasing excitation wavelength can be attributed to the presence of multiple emissive channels with different energy gaps within the PN-CQDs. This phenomenon is commonly observed in carbon quantum dots due to their heterogeneous nature, which includes various surface states and quantum confinement effects [26,27]. The increase in PL intensity up to an excitation wavelength of 440 nm suggests that the highly populated emissive sites are optimally excited at this wavelength, leading to maximum emission.

The pH-dependent PL emission spectrum of the PN-CQDs was studied by varying the pH from 1 to 12 using a calculated amount of nitric acid (HNO3) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH). The PL intensity was found to be maximum at pH 5 as shown in Fig. 5(b) and decreased in both acidic and basic conditions. The pH-dependent behaviour of PL intensity can be explained by the changes in the surface states and protonation/deprotonation of functional groups on the PN-CQDs. In a more acidic environment (below pH 5), the surface carboxyl and hydroxyl groups are protonated, leading to quenching of PL intensity due to non-radiative recombination processes. While increasing pH (up to 5), these groups become deprotonated, suppress the non-radiative recombination and result in maximum PL intensity. Although, above pH 5, decrease trend in PL intensity is observed, which can be attributed to the formation of negatively charged species on the PN-CQDs surface. These negatively charged species lead to increased non-radiative recombination pathways, thereby quenching the PL emission. Additionally, high pH values can cause aggregation of CQDs, further reducing the PL intensity due to the inner filter effect and self-quenching [28].

The excitation and pH-dependent PL studies of PN-CQDs reveal important insights into their optical properties and stability under varying conditions. The excitation-dependent PL behaviour, characterized by a maximum intensity at 440 nm and a red shift in emission, underscores the presence of multiple emissive sites within the dots. The pH-dependent study highlights the sensitivity of PL intensity to surface protonation and deprotonation, with optimal emission observed at pH 5.

By measuring the produced PN-CQDs relative to quinine sulphate (54 %) as a standard, quantum yield (QY) is determined. The fluorescence quantum yield of the sample under study is 56 % and represented by the symbol φ in Eq. (2). The absorption at 350 nm is represented by the symbol A, the emission intensity by I, and the solvents' refractive index by η. Subscripts 'PN-CQDs' and 'Ref' denote the P & N co-doped carbon dots sample under examination and the reference fluorescent dye, respectively.
(2)
φ
PN - C Q D s
=
φ
Ref
I
PN - C Q D s
I
Ref

Ref

PN - C Q D s
η
PN - C Q D s
2

Ref
2

PN-CQDs' photostability was evaluated by exposing the prepared sample to a 2000 lx tungsten bulb as a visible light source and 254 nm UV light for 6 h. The PL intensity was measured at hourly intervals to assess any changes due to prolonged light exposure. When exposed to 254 nm UV light, the PN-CQDs exhibited remarkable photostability, with no significant decrease in PL intensity observed over the 6 h (line A in Fig. 5(c)). This stability under UV light indicates that the PN-CQDs possess a robust structure and surface passivation that protects the emissive states from photodegradation. Under visible light illumination, as indicated by line B in Fig. 5(c), the PN-CQDs exhibited strong photostability, retaining approximately 73 % of their initial PL intensity over the first 4 h. Even after 6 h of continuous exposure, the PN-CQDs sustained a portion of their PL intensity, demonstrating stable performance under prolonged visible light conditions. The slight reduction in PL intensity after 4 h suggests that while the PN-CQDs are generally stable under visible light, prolonged exposure may lead to minimal photobleaching or slight surface modifications. This level of stability is sufficient for most practical applications, including light-emitting devices and sensors, where the material is exposed to ambient or controlled light conditions.

The PL intensity of PN-CQDs was examined with varying concentrations of Na(I) and K(I) ions to assess their ionic stability. As seen from Fig. 5(d), a slight increase in PL intensity was observed with an increase in the concentration of NaCl and KCl from 1 mM to 10 mM, suggesting that the PN-CQDs interact with these ions. This behaviour can be explained by the interaction of Na(I) and K(I) ions with the surface functional groups of the CQDs, potentially leading to the passivation of surface defects and an increase in radiative recombination efficiency. However, it does not necessarily mean that the CQDs are inherently stable in the presence of ions but rather that their PL properties are positively affected by these ions. It highlights the sensitivity of PN-CQDs to changes in their ionic environment. This sensitivity can be harnessed for metal ion sensing applications with similar or higher sensitivity. By tailoring the surface functional groups, the specificity and sensitivity of PN-CQDs towards different metal ions can be optimized. In such applications, the interaction of metal ions with the surface functional groups of CQDs leads to detectable changes in PL intensity, which can be used as a measurable signal. The ability to detect metal ions based on changes in PL intensity makes PN-CQDs promising candidates for applications in environmental monitoring, water quality assessment, and biomedical diagnostics.

From the previous discussion on the fluorescence properties, efficiency and stability of prepared PN-CQDs, the PN-CQDs are tested for metal ion sensing and real water samples application. The sensitivity and selectivity of PN-CQDs for detecting various metal ions were investigated using a range of metal ions: Cu(II), Mn(II), Cr(III), Zn(II), Ba(II), Ag(III), Mg(II), Ca(II), and Li(I) with 5 pH, and 440 nm excitation wavelength. The sensitivity study revealed that the PL intensity of PN-CQDs decreased in the presence of all tested metal ions, indicating a general quenching effect. However, the extent of quenching varied among the different ions (Fig. 6 (a)). Notably, Fe(III) and Hg(II) ions exhibited the most significant quenching effect, suggesting a higher sensitivity of PN-CQDs to these specific ions. In the selectivity study, the PL response of PN-CQDs was evaluated in the presence of ferric and mercuric ions, both individually and in combination with other heavy metal ions. The results given in Fig. 6(b) showed that the PL response of the PN-CQDs in the presence of Fe(III) and other metal ions was similar to the response in the presence of Fe(III) ions alone. Similarly, the response in the presence of mercuric and other metal ions was similar to the response in the presence of Hg(II) ions alone. In the case of dual heavy metal Fe(III) and Hg(II) ions presence, the PL response was similar to the response with only Hg(II) ions. This indicates that mercuric ions dominate the quenching effect in the presence of both ions, suggesting a stronger binding affinity interaction of PN-CQDs with Hg(II) compared to Fe(III). The higher quenching efficiency of Hg(II) ions, possibly related to their larger ionic radius, higher atomic number, and stronger interaction with the CQDs' surface functional groups, making them easily detectable in complex mixtures [29–31]. The sensitivity and significant quenching effect observed for Fe(III) and Hg(II) ions highlight the potential of PN-CQDs as a fluorescent sensor for these metal ions.

A detailed analysis was conducted by incrementally adding calculated concentrations of Fe(III) and Hg(II) ions, ranging from 5 nM to 1000 nM, to the sensing probe. The experimental procedure is outlined in detail in the experimental section. As illustrated in Fig. 7 (a & c), a successive increase in the concentration of Fe(III) and Hg(II) ions led to a continuous decrease in the PL intensity of PN-CQDs, indicating a fluorescence quenching or turn-off sensing mechanism. Moreover, the PL quenching effect is also visible to the naked eye. Image given in Fig. 7(b) clearly shows the diminishing green fluorescence of PN-CQDs in the presence of Fe(III) and Hg(II) ions under UV light in a UV chamber. Thus, results show the effective quenching of PL in presence of Fe(III) and Hg(II) ions. The PL intensity ratio (F/F0) at an excitation wavelength of 440 nm was plotted against the concentration of Fe(III) and Hg(II) ions, as shown in Fig. 7(d & e). The results revealed a linear response range from 10 nM to 50 nM for Fe(III) (R2 = 0.99786) and from 5 nM to 100 nM for Hg(II) (R2 = 0.98587). The fitted linear equations derived from these plots are:
(3)
F
Fo
= - 0.00244
C
Fe
+
[ 0.87 ± 0.001 ]
(4)
F
Fo
= - 0.00126
C
Hg
+
[ 0.46 ± 0.002 ]
Here, F0 and F represent the PL intensities in the absence and presence of Fe(III) and Hg(II) ions, respectively, while CFe and CHg denote the concentrations of Fe(III) and Hg(II) ions. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) was determined by 3.3σ/K (n = 3) and 10σ/ K (n = 3), respectively. Here, σ represents the standard error of the y-intercept, and K denotes the slope of the linear calibration plot (Fig. 7(d & e)), which is also referred to as the fluorescence quenching constant. The calculated values of LOD and LOQ for Fe(III) are 1.35 nM and 4.09 nM, respectively, while for Hg(II), they are 5.23 nM and 15.87 nM, respectively. The higher sensitivity towards Fe(III), can be explained by the easier transfer of electron from the excited state of PN-CQDs to the semi-filled 3d orbitals of Fe(III) due to greater thermodynamic affinity of Fe(III) and the faster coordination process, which promotes the recombination of nonradiative electron-hole pairs, resulting in fluorescence quenching [32].

PN-CQDs can sense ferric and mercuric ions based on changes in PL emission intensity. The sensing mechanism involves PL quenching, which can be analysed through various characterization techniques. Here, we discuss the plausible sensing mechanism considering a linear Stern-Volmer plot, unchanged PL lifetime, and changes in zeta potential.

Hence it can be stated that the sensing mechanism of PN-CQDs for Fe(III) and Hg(II) ions is primarily based on static quenching. The metal ions interact with the PN-CQDs, neutralizing their negative surface charge and forming ground-state complexes. This interaction results in a linear decrease in PL emission intensity without altering the PL lifetime, confirmed by the Stern-Volmer plot and TRPL data. The change in zeta potential further supports the formation of these complexes, providing a comprehensive understanding of the sensing process.

The Table 3 presents a comparison of various quantum dots synthesized using different carbon sources, dopants, and preparation methodologies, targeting specific metal ions for sensing applications. The listed doped carbon quantum dots are designed to detect metal ions such as Cu(II), Fe(III), Hg(II), and ascorbic acid. The element sources and synthesis methods used in these studies reflect a variety of approaches to enhance the performance of CQDs for metal ion sensing. For instance, N, S-CQDs synthesized from chitosan, citric acid, and thiourea at 200 °C for 6 h achieved an impressive limit of detection (LOD) of 0.004 µM for mercuric ions, demonstrating the effectiveness of this methodology in enhancing sensitivity [33]. The table also highlights the range of metal ions targeted by these CQDs, with Fe(III) and Hg(II) being the most commonly studied due to their environmental and biological significance. Notably, our prepared PN-CQDs synthesized from sweet lime juice, ethylenediamine (EDA), and phosphoric acid (PA) via microwave treatment demonstrated excellent sensitivity for Fe(III) and Hg(II) ions, with a competitive LOD compared to other reported CQDs.

The PN-CQDs synthesized in this study stand out for several reasons. First, the LOD for Fe(III) and Hg(II) ions is on par with or better than many of the previously reported CQDs, indicating their high sensitivity. These advantages position PN-CQDs as a promising candidate for practical applications in metal ion detection, particularly for Fe(III) and Hg(II) ions, which are of critical importance in environmental and health-related fields.

Given that the previous experiments were conducted using DI water, the reliability of the sensing assay is assessed for real water samples. The method is adopted from our previous article [18]. Initially, the presence of Fe(III)/Hg(II) in samples of well water, pond water, and seawater was checked using the standard assay used before. The results, as depicted in Fig. 9 , revealed that the fluorescence intensity of PN-CQDs in well, pond, and seawater was quite similar (sustained more than 90 %) to that in DI water. To evaluate the assay's practicality with real water samples, we introduced spiked solutions with three different concentrations of Fe(III) and Hg(II) using each of the well, pond, and sea water samples separately. Then utilizing the calibration curve established earlier (Fig. 7(d & e)), the concentration of ions in the real water samples was calculated and compared with the actual concentrations of the spikes. Optimistically, the assay demonstrated a high level of recovery across all spiked solutions given in Table 4 . Recovery percentages were obtained using Eq. (5), and the outcomes are given in the table:
(5)
Recovery % =
C a
-
C b
C s
x 100
where Cs is the spiked concentrations of Fe(III)/Hg(II) in NCDs, Ca, and Cb are concentrations of Fe(III)/Hg(II) in sea, pond, and well water after and before adding the standard Fe(III)/Hg(II). Notably, the best recoveries for both ions were found in pond and seawater for the 40 nM spiked solution. These recovery rates highlight the assay's effectiveness for detecting Fe(III) and Hg(II) in real-world applications.

In summary, fluorescent phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (PN-CQDs) were successfully synthesized using a microwave irradiation method with Citrus limetta juice as the carbon precursor, phosphoric acid (85 % concentration) as the phosphorus source, and ethylenediamine as the nitrogen source. Structural and chemical characterizations confirmed the formation of spherical particles with an amorphous carbon core structure and rich surface functionalities, measuring approximately 4 nm in size. XPS analysis indicated a doping level of 6.63 % phosphorus and 15.48 % nitrogen in the PN-CQDs. Optical studies revealed that the prepared PN-CQDs contained aromatic domains and various surface functional groups, resulting in excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) over UV and visible excitation wavelengths. The highest fluorescence emission intensity, corresponding to a wavelength of 550 nm, was observed with 440 nm excitation. The PN-CQDs exhibited a high quantum yield (56 %) and strong photostability under continuous UV and visible light exposure for more than four hours.

Furthermore, the potential of the synthesized PN-CQDs as heavy metal ion sensors was investigated for Fe(III) and Hg(II) ions. The PN-CQDs displayed higher selectivity for Hg(II) ions and greater sensitivity for Fe(III) ions in the presence of other metal ions. Titration experiments revealed a detection limit of 1.35 nM for Fe(III) and 5.23 nM for Hg(II) ions. The quenching mechanism was attributed to static quenching due to ground state complex ions pair formation through interactions between the P, N-doped carbon surface structure and the metal ions. The incorporation of phosphorus and pyridinic-N groups in the PN-CQDs contributed to the development of sensitive probes for the selective detection of Fe(III) and Hg(II) in water samples, demonstrating good reliability and recovery rates of approximately 96–100 %.

Rajnee Yadav: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Vikas Lahariya: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Akhilesh Kumar Singh: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An “on-off-on” fluorescent nanoprobe for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on phosphorus/nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dots
Anal. Chim. Acta.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Gong, Y. Liu, Z. Yang, S. Shuang, Z. Zhang, and C. Dong, “An ‘on-off-on’ fluorescent nanoprobe for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on phosphorus/nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dot,” Volume 968, Pages 85 - 96, vol. 968, pp. 85–96, May 2017, doi: 10.1016/j.aca.2017.02.038.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile, rapid synthesis of N,P-dual-doped carbon dots as a label-free multifunctional nanosensor for Mn(VII) detection, temperature sensing and cellular imaging
Sens Actuators B Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Du, G. Li, X. Gong, G. Zhonghui, S. Shuang, M Xian, and C. Dong, “Facile, rapid synthesis of N,P-dual-doped carbon dots as a label-free multifunctional nanosensor for Mn(VII) detection, temperature sensing and cellular imaging,” Sens Actuators B Chem, vol. 277, pp. 492–501, Dec. 2018, doi: 10.1016/J.SNB.2018.09.027.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly Fe3+-Selective Fluorescent Nanoprobe Based on Ultrabright N/P Codoped Carbon Dots and Its Application in Biological Samples
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Shangguan, J. Huang, D. He, X. He, K. Wang, R. Ye, X. Yang, T. Qing, and J. Tang, “Highly Fe3+-Selective Fluorescent Nanoprobe Based on Ultrabright N/P Codoped Carbon Dots and Its Application in Biological Samples,” Anal Chem, vol. 89, no. 14, pp. 7477–7484, Jul. 2017, doi: 10.1021/ACS.ANALCHEM.7B01053.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Rajnee Yadav is a researcher specializing in carbon-based nanomaterials for development of sustainable and multifunctional nanomaterials for environmental applications. She pursued her PhD from the Department of Physics, Amity School of Applied Sciences, Amity University Haryana, India, under the supervision of Dr. Vikas Lahariya. Her doctoral work focused on the synthesis and spectroscopic characterization of non-metal doped and co-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for metal ion sensing and their applicability in real-world water samples. Her research explores the photophysical properties of CQDs, their ionic, pH and photo stability, and their application in developing fluorescence-based sensors for metal ions, including their detection in real water samples. She has contributed to multiple studies on fluorescence-based sensing, ionic stability, and the environmental applications of nanomaterials. Rajnee has published her research in reputed peer-reviewed international journals and has also contributed to book chapters on carbon-based quantum dots, focusing on their sensing applications. Her work has been presented at various national and international conferences.

Dr. Vikas Lahariya completed her Ph.D. in Physics in 2011 from Rani Durgawati University (RDVV) Jabalpur, M.P., India. Dr. Vikas Lahariya is working as an Assistant Professor in the Department of Physics, Amity University Haryana, India. Her research primarily focuses on experimental solid-state physics and materials science, with expertise in photoluminescence spectroscopy and electroluminescence. She has worked extensively on semiconductor nanostructures, carbon quantum dots, aluminates, sulfides, and metal oxide nanomaterials and their heterostructures, investigating their structural, optical, and electronic properties through various techniques. Her research also explores applications in photonic and optoelectronic devices, with particular emphasis on optimizing energy-efficient lighting, devices using nanophosphors and luminescent nanomaterials. Recently, her work has extended to fluorometric sensing and detection of heavy metal ions in water and soil. Dr. V. Lahariya has published more than 30 research articles, reviews, and 7 book chapters in reputable SCI/SCOPUS peer-reviewed journals and indexed books respectively. She is co-investigator for an Indian patent. She has been recognized with several awards for outstanding research and best researcher by reputed organizations.“

Akhilesh Kumar Singh is working as an Assistant Professor at the Department of Physical Sciences, Banasthali Vidyapith, Rajasthan, India. He holds an M.Sc. in Physics (2004) from V.B.S. Purvanchal University, Jaunpur, India, and an M. Tech. (2007) and Ph.D. (2012) in Materials Science and Technology from Indian Institute of Technology (Banaras Hindu University) Varanasi, India. He worked as a Postdoctoral Fellow at the UNAM, México, and the University of Strathclyde, Glasgow. His research interests include hybrid halide perovskites, luminescent materials for energy harvesting, luminescent security inks, etc. He has published over 50 peer-reviewed international journal papers, authored 4 book chapters, and is a co-investigator of an Indian Patent.
